title: Guided Questions and Classroom Practice
subtitle: From Understanding to Implementation
summary: Bridges professional knowledge into classroom practice. Teachers learn to use guided (mediated) questions to lead inquiry without prescribing answers, design investigation routines across developmental bands, and implement meaning-first instruction in neurodivergent-informed ways. Emphasizes assessment as consolidation and learner agency as a design goal.
learning_outcomes: Design and use guided questions that mediate learning without giving answers
Plan SWI inquiry routines appropriate for specific developmental bands
Implement meaning-first instruction that supports neurodivergent learners
Use assessment as consolidation rather than testing
Adapt pacing, scaffolding, and entry points to support learner variability
Build classroom cultures of investigation where students are positioned as capable
key_concepts: Multiple entry points for the same investigation
swi_connections: Applies all Four Questions in classroom contexts
Uses word sums, matrices, and join analysis as teaching tools
Positions the SWI inquiry cycle as a daily classroom routine
int_connections: Translates INT principles into specific classroom practices
Addresses executive function through predictable routines
Reduces cognitive load through scaffolded inquiry
Supports regulation through bounded, coherent tasks
Eliminates sensory surprises and gamified noise

sections.title: The question that doesn't answer itself
sections.summary: Guided (mediated) questions open investigation without foreclosing it — they are not hints, not Socratic irony, and not leading questions. The same classroom moment handled three ways: telling, hinting, and guiding. What the guided version produces that the other two do not, cognitively and affectively.

sections.title: Adapting investigation across developmental bands
sections.summary: The Four Questions and the inquiry cycle apply from K-1 through 9-12, but complexity and scaffolding differ by band. K-1: single-base words, simple suffixes, word families of 3–5. 2-3: bound bases, prefixes, small matrices. 4-8: multi-base words, etymological layers, full matrices. 9-12: disciplinary vocabulary, student-led investigation, extended word histories.

sections.title: Designing for neurodivergent learners
sections.summary: How meaning-first instruction structurally supports students with language-based learning differences, ADHD, and sensory processing needs — not as accommodation but as design. Predictable routines reduce executive function load. Principled explanations replace arbitrary memorization. The absence of gamified noise and timed performance is an affordance, not a compromise.

sections.title: Assessment as consolidation
sections.summary: The written reflections in this course are consolidation tools, not tests. Applying this model to classroom practice: low-stakes formative tasks that require students to explain, not recall. The gate criteria in each module as a model of what consolidating assessment looks like. How to write criteria that distinguish principled understanding from surface compliance.

sections.title: Multiple entry points for the same investigation
sections.summary: Not every student enters a word investigation from the same place. Designing tasks with multiple entry points: the student who starts with meaning, the one who starts with a related word they know, the one who notices a grapheme. How to hold the investigation open while students find their way in — without lowering the cognitive target.

sections.title: Assumed competence in practice
sections.summary: Assumed competence is a pedagogical stance, not a compliment. It shows up in task design (the investigation is structured so students can succeed), in language (errors are treated as evidence of reasoning), and in pacing (the response to a wrong answer is another question, not a correction). Concrete classroom examples.

sections.title: Building the investigation culture: the first four weeks
sections.summary: Practical sequence for introducing SWI routines into a classroom that hasn't used them. Week 1: teacher-led Four Questions investigation as a class. Week 2: small-group investigations with modeled guided questions. Weeks 3-4: students begin raising their own word questions. What to expect, what to hold lightly, and how to know it's working.

gate_criteria: Guided questions are distinguished from hints and leading questions by what they point toward: a process, not a predetermined answer
The Four Questions are described as constant across K-12, with word complexity — not the framework — varying by developmental band
Assumed competence is articulated as a design stance that structures tasks for engagement, not as avoiding correction
Assessment as consolidation is described in terms of what it requires students to do — explain, apply, investigate — not recall under pressure
